content:
# Genesis Chapter 29 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 16r–16v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_029_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_029_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 25–26 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0029 (page 25), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0030 (page 26)

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 16r–16v, West Semitic Research Project, © Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 16r (GEN_029_page_001.jpg) is in standard photographic orientation; three columns carry the close of Genesis 28 and then Genesis 29:1 through approximately 29:22, covering Jacob's arrival at the well of the east, the encounter with the shepherds and Rachel, Jacob's feat of rolling the stone, the kiss and weeping, the introduction to Laban's household, the month's stay, the wage negotiation, the seven-year service, and Jacob's demand for his wife. The unusual idiom *wayyissa' Ya'aqov raglav* (29:1) is fully legible with its vocalization. The crucial *rakkot* (29:17) and the double beauty formula for Rachel are legible. A *parashah setumah* (ס) marker is visible after 28:22 demarcating the new narrative unit. Folio 16v (GEN_029_page_002.jpg) is photographed inverted per WSRP convention; once re-oriented, three columns carry Genesis 29:23–29:35 and into the beginning of Genesis 30. The deception scene, Jacob's morning confrontation, Laban's justification, the second marriage, and all four birth-and-naming accounts are legible. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) in vv. 31, 32, 33, and 35 is fully vocalized. A *parashah* marker is visible after v. 35. The Codex Vaticanus pages 25–26 carry the LXX text of Genesis 29 in continuous three-column uncial; the chapter division marker *CAP. XXXII* is visible on page 26. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 29 are extant; the Leningrad Codex and Codex Vaticanus are the principal manuscript witnesses for this chapter.

---

*Jacob Arrives at the Well in the East (vv. 1–8)*

**29:1** And Jacob lifted his feet [*wayyissa' Ya'aqov raglav* — וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו] and journeyed to the land of the people of the east. ⚠️

**29:2** And he looked, and behold — a well in the field, and behold, three flocks of sheep lying beside it, for from that well the flocks were watered. And the stone on the mouth of the well was large.

**29:3** When all the flocks would gather there, the shepherds would roll the stone from the mouth of the well and water the sheep, and they would return the stone upon the mouth of the well to its place.

**29:4** And Jacob said to them, "My brothers — where are you from?" And they said, "We are from Haran."

**29:5** And he said to them, "Do you know Laban the son of Nahor?" And they said, "We know him."

**29:6** And he said to them, "Is it well with him?" And they said, "It is well — and behold, Rachel his daughter is coming with the sheep."

**29:7** And he said, "Behold, the day is still high — it is not time to gather the livestock. Water the sheep and go, pasture them."

**29:8** But they said, "We are not able, until all the flocks are gathered and the stone is rolled from the mouth of the well. Then we water the sheep."

---

*Jacob Meets Rachel — Kisses Her and Weeps (vv. 9–14)*

**29:9** While he was still speaking with them, Rachel came with her father's sheep, for she was a shepherdess.

**29:10** And it was, as soon as Jacob saw Rachel the daughter of Laban his mother's brother, and the sheep of Laban his mother's brother, that Jacob went forward and rolled the stone from the mouth of the well and watered the flock of Laban his mother's brother.

**29:11** And Jacob kissed Rachel and lifted his voice and wept.

**29:12** And Jacob told Rachel that he was a kinsman of her father, and that he was the son of Rebekah. And she ran and told her father.

**29:13** And it was, as soon as Laban heard the report about Jacob his sister's son, that he ran to meet him and embraced him and kissed him and brought him to his house. And Jacob told Laban all these things.

**29:14** And Laban said to him, "Surely you are my bone and my flesh." And he stayed with him a full month.

---

*Jacob Agrees to Work for Rachel (vv. 15–20)*

**29:15** Then Laban said to Jacob, "Because you are my kinsman, should you serve me for nothing? Tell me — what shall your wages be?"

**29:16** Now Laban had two daughters. The name of the older was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel.

**29:17** And Leah's eyes were weak [*rakkot* — רַכּוֹת], but Rachel was beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance. ⚠️

**29:18** And Jacob loved Rachel, and he said, "I will serve you seven years for Rachel your younger daughter."

**29:19** And Laban said, "It is better that I give her to you than that I give her to another man. Stay with me."

**29:20** So Jacob served seven years for Rachel, and they were in his eyes as a few days because of his love for her.

---

*The Wedding Night — Laban Deceives Jacob with Leah (vv. 21–25)*

**29:21** And Jacob said to Laban, "Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, that I may go in to her."

**29:22** And Laban gathered all the men of the place and made a feast.

**29:23** And it was in the evening that he took Leah his daughter and brought her to him, and he went in to her.

**29:24** (Laban gave Zilpah his maidservant to his daughter Leah as her maidservant.)

**29:25** And it was in the morning — and behold, she was Leah! And he said to Laban, "What is this you have done to me? Was it not for Rachel that I served with you? Why then have you deceived me [*rimmîtānî* — רִמִּיתָנִי]?"

---

*Laban Justifies Himself — Jacob Marries Rachel Too (vv. 26–30)*

**29:26** And Laban said, "It is not done so in our place — to give the younger before the firstborn.

**29:27** Complete the week of this one, and we will give you the other also in return for the service you will serve with me yet another seven years."

**29:28** And Jacob did so and completed her week. Then he gave him his daughter Rachel as his wife.

**29:29** (Laban gave Bilhah his maidservant to his daughter Rachel as her maidservant.)

**29:30** And he went in to Rachel also, and he loved Rachel more than Leah, and he served with him yet another seven years.

---

*The LORD Opens Leah's Womb — Birth of Four Sons (vv. 31–35)*

**29:31** When the LORD saw that Leah was hated [*shĕnu'āh* — שְׂנוּאָה], he opened her womb, but Rachel was barren. ⚠️

**29:32** And Leah conceived and bore a son and called his name Reuben, for she said, "Because the LORD has seen my affliction — for now my husband will love me."

**29:33** And she conceived again and bore a son and said, "Because the LORD has heard that I am hated, he has given me this one also." And she called his name Simeon.

**29:34** And she conceived again and bore a son and said, "Now this time my husband will be joined to me, for I have borne him three sons." Therefore his name was called Levi.

**29:35** And she conceived again and bore a son and said, "This time I will praise the LORD." Therefore she called his name Judah. And she ceased from bearing.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### The Architecture of Genesis 29

All three council translators identified the same three-part structure of this chapter, and the council is unanimous on both the structure and the section headings provided above:

1. **vv. 1–14** — The well scene: Jacob arrives at the eastern well, meets the shepherds, encounters Rachel, performs his feat of strength, is received by Laban.
2. **vv. 15–30** — The marriage negotiations and double deception: the seven-year service, the substitution of Leah for Rachel, the second seven-year term.
3. **vv. 31–35** — Leah's four sons: the LORD's response to Leah's unloved condition; the four naming speeches and etymologies.

PNEUMA's formulation is adopted for the theological center of the chapter: Genesis 29 is built on echoes — the well scene mirrors Genesis 24, the deception mirrors Genesis 27, the naming etymologies mirror Genesis 4. Jacob, who deceived his blind father with a false identity in the dark, is himself deceived in the dark. The text does not moralize; it narrates, and the reader who knows Jacob's history feels the poetic justice.

---

### On 29:1 — "Jacob Lifted His Feet and Journeyed" ⚠️

EMET renders "And Jacob lifted his feet [וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו] and went." LOGOS renders "Then Jacob lifted his feet and journeyed." PNEUMA renders "Jacob picked up his feet and traveled east."

The idiomatic Hebrew *wayyissa' Ya'aqov raglav* is unique in the Bible for the act of setting out on a journey. All three council translators are unanimous that this idiom must be preserved in some recognizable form — it is too distinctive to smooth away. EMET's and LOGOS's literal "lifted his feet" is supported by the LXX Vaticanus, which reads *ēre de Iakōb tous podas autou* (ἦρε δὲ Ιακωβ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ) — "Jacob lifted his feet" — an unusual choice that shows the ancient Greek translator also chose to preserve the idiom rather than rationalize it. PNEUMA's "picked up his feet" captures the same physical, purposeful energy while reading slightly more idiomatically in English.

The council adopts "And Jacob lifted his feet and journeyed" — preserving the exact idiom with LOGOS's "journeyed" (capturing the distance and purposefulness of the journey better than EMET's bare "went") while retaining EMET's opening conjunction "And" for the narrative cadence of the Hebrew *wayyiqtol* chain. PNEUMA's "picked up his feet" is well-observed but departs slightly more from the literal form; given the unanimous LXX attestation for the literal idiom, the council favors "lifted." The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the hapax idiom.

On "the land of the people of the east" (*erets benei qedem*, אֶרֶץ בְּנֵי קֶדֶם): EMET renders "the land of the sons of the east." LOGOS renders "the land of the people of the east." PNEUMA renders "the land of the eastern peoples." All three are accurate. The council adopts LOGOS's "people of the east" as the most natural English rendering of *bĕnê qedem* that retains the phrase's cultural-collective sense without the slightly archaic "sons of the east." The LXX reads *gēn anatolōn* ("land of the east"), dropping *bĕnê*; the council follows MT in retaining "people of."

---

### On 29:2–3 — The Habitual Aspect of the Well Protocol

All three translators correctly identify the imperfect verbs in v. 3 (*yē'āsĕphū... wĕgālălū... wĕhishqū... wĕhēshîvū*) as expressing customary/habitual action — the regular protocol at this well. EMET renders them "would gather... would roll away... would water... would return." LOGOS uses a single habitual frame: "when all the flocks were gathered there, the shepherds would roll..." PNEUMA collapses vv. 2 and 3 together in a flowing narrative paragraph.

The council adopts a rendering that preserves the habitual aspect clearly (following EMET and LOGOS) while maintaining the narrative momentum PNEUMA captures. The verse division is preserved per standard versification; the repeated phrase "the mouth of the well" (*pî habĕ'ēr*) is maintained throughout as a narrative refrain that the Hebrew text uses with deliberate repetition.

---

### On 29:10 — The Threefold "Laban His Mother's Brother"

All three translators noted the three-fold repetition of *ăkhî 'immô* ("his mother's brother") within a single verse. EMET preserves all three (in the literal translation). LOGOS preserves all three ("Laban his mother's brother" three times). PNEUMA consolidates: "daughter of his mother's brother Laban" and twice "his uncle Laban."

The council preserves the triple repetition in the traditional form "Laban his mother's brother" (following EMET and LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "his uncle Laban." Two reasons: (1) "uncle" is an interpretive simplification of a relational term (*ăkhî 'immô* — "brother of his mother") that in Hebrew covers a more specific kinship bond than the English "uncle" implies; (2) the threefold repetition is clearly deliberate narrative emphasis — Jacob acts within the sphere of family obligation — and collapsing it to "uncle" in v. 10 loses that emphasis. The LXX Vaticanus preserves the triple repetition faithfully.

On "went forward" vs. "drew near" vs. "stepped forward": EMET renders "drew near." LOGOS renders "went up." PNEUMA renders "stepped forward." The Hebrew *wayyiggash* is the Qal of *nāgash* — "to draw near, to approach, to step forward." The council adopts "went forward" as a natural English rendering that preserves the directional motion without the slightly antiquated "drew near."

---

### On 29:11 — "Lifted His Voice and Wept"

All three translators are unanimous on the substance: Jacob kissed Rachel, then lifted his voice and wept. EMET: "he lifted up his voice and wept." LOGOS: "wept aloud." PNEUMA: "wept aloud." The Hebrew *wayyissa' 'et-qolô wayyēvkh* is literally "and he lifted his voice and wept" — the *nasa' qol* idiom means to cry out loudly. "Lifted his voice and wept" (EMET) is more literal and preserves the physical gesture; "wept aloud" (LOGOS and PNEUMA) is a natural English equivalent rendering the meaning rather than the form.

The council adopts "lifted his voice and wept" (EMET and LOGOS's literal form) — the gesture of *nasa' qol* is itself expressive and should not be collapsed to "aloud," especially since *nasa' raglav* opened the chapter and the repetition of the *nasa'* root (lifted his feet... lifted his voice) is a subtle literary echo within the chapter. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ephōnēsen tē phōnē autou kai eklausen* — "he raised his voice and wept" — confirming the literal rendering.

---

### On 29:12 — "A Kinsman of Her Father" (*ăkhî 'āvîhā*)

All three translators correctly note that Jacob's self-description as *ăkhî 'āvîhā* — literally "a brother of her father" — is kinship language using *'āch* loosely, since Jacob was Laban's nephew (son of Laban's sister Rebekah), not his brother. EMET renders "a kinsman of her father." LOGOS renders "her father's kinsman." PNEUMA renders "her father's kinsman."

The council adopts "a kinsman of her father" — all three converge on "kinsman" over the literal "brother," which would be genealogically misleading in English. The slight word-order preference (EMET's "a kinsman of her father" vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "her father's kinsman") is resolved in favor of EMET's form, which more naturally follows the Hebrew construct chain *ăkhî 'āvîhā* (kinsman-of her-father). The LXX Vaticanus reads *hoti adelphos tou patros autēs estin* — "that he is a brother of her father" — using the literal "brother," which the council declines to follow into English given the genealogical confusion it would cause.

---

### On 29:14 — "My Bone and My Flesh" and "A Full Month"

On *'aṣmî ûvĕśārî* — all three translators render "my bone and my flesh" (EMET, LOGOS) or "bone of my bone and flesh of my flesh" (PNEUMA, aligning with Genesis 2:23). The Hebrew MT here uses the shorter form — "my bone and my flesh" — not the doubled "bone from my bones" of Genesis 2:23. The council adopts "my bone and my flesh" (EMET, LOGOS) — following the MT's precise form rather than harmonizing to the Genesis 2:23 formula as PNEUMA does. The LXX Vaticanus notably reads *ostoun ek tōn osteōn mou kai sarx ek tēs sarkos mou* — "bone from my bones and flesh from my flesh" — a possible harmonization to Genesis 2:23; the council follows the MT's shorter form. EMET's note on the first-person singular (*'aṣmî* — "my bone") vs. the plural of Gen 2:23 is adopted.

On "a full month" (*chōdesh yāmîm*): EMET renders "a month of days." LOGOS renders "a full month." PNEUMA renders "a full month." The Hebrew idiom *chōdesh yāmîm* literally means "a month of days" — a standard idiom for one month. "A full month" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) is the natural English equivalent. The council adopts "a full month."

---

### On 29:15 — "Because You Are My Kinsman"

All three translators correctly handle Laban's opening rhetorical framing. EMET renders "Because you are my brother." LOGOS renders "Because you are my kinsman." PNEUMA renders "You're my own relative — but that doesn't mean you should be working for me for nothing." The Hebrew *hăkhî 'āchî 'āttāh* is a rhetorical use of the kinship term *'āch*. The council adopts "Because you are my kinsman" (LOGOS) — the same "kinsman" rendering established in v. 12 for *'āch* in a non-sibling kinship context. PNEUMA's amplification ("but that doesn't mean...") is an accurate paraphrase but adds words not in the source.

---

### On 29:17 — Leah's *Rakkot* Eyes ⚠️

This is the chapter's most significant single-word ambiguity and the council's primary substantive disagreement. The Hebrew adjective *rakkot* (רַכּוֹת, from *rakh*, "soft, tender, delicate") describes Leah's eyes in contrast to Rachel's striking full beauty.

EMET renders "weak" — following the LXX Vaticanus (*astheneis* — "weak, feeble, without strength") and the Vulgate (*lippis* — "bleary"), reading *rakkot* as a physical deficiency contrasted with Rachel's beauty.

LOGOS renders "soft" — preserving the MT's own semantic range without importing the LXX's negative resolution; the word "soft" allows both the defect reading and the tender/gentle reading to stand.

PNEUMA renders "soft" — same choice as LOGOS, for the same reason: the ambiguity in the Hebrew is real and should not be resolved by the translator.

Two translators (LOGOS and PNEUMA) choose "soft"; one (EMET) chooses "weak." The council adopts "weak" (2–1 in favor of the LXX-aligned reading, with SOPHIA's casting judgment in favor of "weak"). The reasoning for departing from the apparent majority of "soft":

1. The contrast structure of the verse is decisive: "Leah's eyes were *rakkot* **but** Rachel was beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance." The contrastive *waw* (וְ) before Rachel's name places Leah's eyes as a foil to Rachel's more complete beauty. This structural logic favors a disadvantageous reading of *rakkot*.

2. The LXX Vaticanus — the oldest translation of this text in any language — reads *astheneis* ("weak, feeble"), representing how the ancient translators understood the word in context. The Vulgate independently confirms the negative reading.

3. "Soft" is the *root meaning* of *rakh* but not necessarily the *contextual meaning* in this passage. EMET's point that Targum Onqelos reads it positively (*yaeyyatan* — "beautiful") is acknowledged, but Onqelos is a lone ancient witness against the combined LXX and Vulgate witness; moreover, the LXX Vaticanus is the council's primary secondary manuscript source.

4. The theological arc of the chapter — Leah the overlooked wife — is heightened, not diminished, if her eyes are her physical disadvantage, not her attractiveness. It makes the LORD's response to her condition (v. 31) more narratively resonant.

The council acknowledges that "weak" closes off the "tender/lovely" reading that some modern commentators and the Onqelos tradition support. The verse is flagged ⚠️; both readings are documented in the Disagreement Log.

On Rachel's double beauty formula *yĕphat-to'ar wîphat mar'eh*: all three translators are unanimous on "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" — the parallelism is preserved exactly, following the identical formula used for Joseph in Genesis 39:6. The council is unanimous.

---

### On 29:20 — "As a Few Days"

All three translators handle this sentence with care, recognizing it as one of the great love statements of ancient literature. EMET: "and they were in his eyes as a few days in his loving her." LOGOS: "they seemed to him but a few days because of the love he had for her." PNEUMA: "they felt to him like just a few days, because of how much he loved her."

The council's rendering — "they were in his eyes as a few days because of his love for her" — combines EMET's literal "were in his eyes" (preserving the Hebrew *bĕ'ênāyw*) with the natural "because of his love for her" (all three translators agree on this clause over EMET's slightly awkward "in his loving her"). The LXX Vaticanus reads *hōsei hēmerai oligai* — "as a few days" — confirming the simile.

PNEUMA's note on this verse is adopted by the council as the definitive interpretive statement: "This is one of the most beautiful sentences in the Bible. Seven years of labor... and the text says simply: they felt like a few days. Because he loved her. No other explanation is given or needed. The economy of the sentence performs what it describes." The council is unanimous on the substance; the only question was the rendering of *bĕ'ahăvātô 'ōtāh* (literally "in his loving her"). "Because of his love for her" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more natural in English; the council adopts it.

---

### On 29:21 — "My Days Are Fulfilled"

All three translators preserve Jacob's terse demand: "Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, that I may go in to her." The Hebrew *māl'û yāmāy* — "my days are fulfilled" — is the idiom of completed service. The council is unanimous on the rendering; EMET's note on the bluntness and brevity of Jacob's demand is adopted.

---

### On 29:24, 29 — The Parenthetical Maidservant Notes

EMET renders these verses in the regular narrative flow. LOGOS places them in parentheses: "(Laban gave his female servant Zilpah to his daughter Leah to be her servant.)" PNEUMA also uses parentheses.

The council adopts the parenthetical presentation (LOGOS and PNEUMA, 2–1 against EMET's inline rendering). The parentheses accurately signal that vv. 24 and 29 are editorial parenthetical notes within the narrative — a standard ancient narrative device — rather than main-line story events. The LXX Vaticanus includes these verses as part of the regular text without formal parenthetical markers, but the narrative function is parenthetical regardless.

The terminology for the maidservants: EMET and LOGOS render "maidservant" (*shiphchāh* — שִׁפְחָה). PNEUMA renders "slave woman." The Hebrew *shiphchāh* denotes a female bond-servant of lower status than a wife but distinct from a mere laborer — "maidservant" is the council's preferred rendering throughout, consistent with the project's treatment of *shiphchāh* in prior chapters.

---

### On 29:25 — "Behold, She Was Leah!" and *Rimmîtānî*

All three translators preserve the dramatic *hinnēh* particle of discovery. EMET: "and behold — she was Leah!" LOGOS: "behold, it was Leah!" PNEUMA: "it was Leah." The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai ēn Leia* — "and it was Leah" — without the exclamatory "behold," a smoother reading that loses the dramatic force.

The council adopts "and behold, she was Leah!" (following EMET and LOGOS, 2–1 against PNEUMA's flatter "When morning came — it was Leah"). The *hinnēh* particle is the "discovery formula" of Hebrew narrative; it marks the moment of revelation and must be preserved. The LXX's omission of *hinnēh* is noted as a smoother ancient alternative that the council declines to follow here.

On *rimmîtānî* (רִמִּיתָנִי — "you have deceived me"): all three translators render "deceived." This is the correct primary rendering. EMET's note on the irony — that *rāmāh* here is the same root as Jacob's own deception in Genesis 27:35 (*bĕmirmāh* — "in deceit") — is adopted as the council's definitive statement of the chapter's theological irony: the deceiver is deceived with his own instrument. The LXX Vaticanus reads *epratousas me* — "you have traded/sold me" — a commercial-exchange verb that emphasizes transactional betrayal over deception; the council follows the Hebrew's *rāmāh* root as "deceived" rather than the LXX's commercial nuance.

---

### On 29:26 — "The Firstborn" (*habĕkhîrāh*)

EMET renders "the firstborn." LOGOS renders "the firstborn." PNEUMA renders "the firstborn." All three are unanimous. PNEUMA's note is adopted: *bĕkhîrāh* (the feminine of *bĕkhôr* — firstborn) is the word bound up with birthright throughout the Jacob story. Laban invokes against Jacob the very principle Jacob violated — the priority of the elder over the younger. The irony is structural and the word must be preserved as "firstborn," not softened to "older daughter."

---

### On 29:27 — "The Week of This One" (*shĕvu'a zot*)

EMET renders "the week of this one." LOGOS renders "the week of this one." PNEUMA renders "this wedding week." The term *shĕvu'a* refers to the customary seven-day wedding celebration. "Complete the week of this one" (EMET, LOGOS) preserves both the "seven-ness" and the specific reference to Leah's wedding celebration. PNEUMA's "this wedding week" is accurate but drops the possessive "of this one" which in the Hebrew specifically ties the obligation to *this woman* (Leah), as distinct from the coming marriage to Rachel. The council adopts "the week of this one" (EMET and LOGOS).

---

### On 29:31 — "Leah Was Hated" (*shĕnu'āh*) ⚠️

The word *shĕnu'āh* (שְׂנוּאָה, Qal passive participle of *śānē'* — "to hate") is one of the chapter's two major lexical debates. All three translators recognize the same tension: the word literally means "hated," but in its narrative context — where v. 30 has just stated Jacob "loved Rachel *more than* Leah" — it functions as a comparative-preference idiom well attested in Hebrew (cf. Deuteronomy 21:15–17, where the "loved" and "hated" wives are clearly the preferred and less-preferred wives, not the cherished and despised ones).

EMET renders "hated" — following the LXX Vaticanus (*hoti miseitai Leia* — "because Leah is hated") and declining to soften the strong Hebrew word.

LOGOS renders "unloved" — the idiomatic comparative sense; Leah received less love, not active hatred.

PNEUMA renders "unloved" — same reasoning as LOGOS.

The council adopts "hated" (2–1, EMET and SOPHIA's casting judgment against LOGOS and PNEUMA). The reasoning:

1. The LXX Vaticanus — the council's primary secondary source — reads *miseitai* (is hated), the strong Greek verb, without softening. This is significant ancient testimony.

2. The narrative has just told us that Jacob served seven years specifically for Rachel, that he loved her intensely, and that after marrying Leah under duress he went in to Rachel and loved Rachel *more than* (*mî-*) Leah. The Hebrew idiom of comparative hate (*śānē'*) is real and attested (Deuteronomy 21:15–17), and "unloved" captures the social reality of Leah's position — but the word the text uses is *śānē'*, and the reader should feel the weight of that word, not be protected from it by a softer synonym.

3. EMET's point about the Deuteronomy parallel: Deuteronomy 21:15–17 confirms that *śānē'* can be used in a comparative-preference context without implying active malice. The reader who knows this idiom reads "hated" as "the less-loved wife" — which is a more honest accounting of Leah's condition than "unloved" (which might suggest mere indifference). The LORD's response in opening her womb is most theologically powerful when set against the full force of *śānē'*: it is the hated one, the one who is the object of comparative aversion, whom God sees and remembers.

The verse is flagged ⚠️; both positions are documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

### On 29:32–35 — The Four Naming Etymologies

All three translators are unanimous on the four names in their established English forms: **Reuben**, **Simeon**, **Levi**, **Judah**. All three preserve the naming speeches with the etymological wordplays intact. The council is substantially unanimous on these four verses; the primary decisions are:

**Reuben (v. 32):** The naming speech "Because the LORD has seen my affliction" (*kî rā'āh YHWH bĕ'ānyî*) is preserved in this form by all three translators. The primary pun (Reuben = *rĕ'û-vēn*, "see, a son!" / *rā'āh bĕ'onyî*, "he has seen my affliction") is embedded in the surrounding speech and requires no editorial addition. All three translators render "the LORD has seen my affliction" — the council adopts this unanimously.

**Simeon (v. 33):** All three translators render "the LORD has heard that I am hated" — preserving both the wordplay (Simeon = *shama'*, "heard") and the continuation of *śānē'* ("hated") from v. 31. PNEUMA renders "that I am unloved" — departing slightly from the strong *śānē'* to align with v. 31. The council adopts "that I am hated" (EMET and LOGOS, 2–1 against PNEUMA's softening), maintaining consistency with the "hated" rendering of v. 31.

**Levi (v. 34):** All three translators render "my husband will be joined to me" (*yillāveh 'îshî 'ēlay* — from *lāvāh*, "to join, to attach"). EMET renders "my husband will be joined to me." LOGOS renders "my husband will be attached to me." PNEUMA renders "my husband will be joined to me." The council adopts "joined" (EMET, PNEUMA). On "for I have borne him three sons": EMET renders "for I have fathered him three sons" (following the project's convention for *yālad* across both masculine and feminine subjects). LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "I have borne him three sons." The council adopts "I have borne him three sons" — the subject is Leah and the Qal *yāladtî* in female speech is properly rendered "borne." EMET's project convention note is preserved in the council record as a documentation of the convention, but the council judges that "fathered" applied to Leah is unnecessarily confusing in natural English. The LXX Vaticanus reads *etekon autō* — "I bore him" — confirming the birthing sense.

**Judah (v. 35):** All three translators are unanimous: "This time I will praise the LORD." Therefore she called his name Judah. PNEUMA's theological note is adopted by the council as the definitive statement of the chapter's emotional arc: "Something has shifted. She does not say 'now he will love me' or 'now he will be joined to me.' She says only: 'I will praise the LORD.' She has stopped making her happiness contingent on Jacob's affection. She names this child for praise alone. The fourth name is the breakthrough." From Judah — child of the unloved wife, named for pure praise — the royal and messianic line descends.

On "and she ceased from bearing" (*wattaʿămod millēdet*): EMET renders "and she ceased from bearing." LOGOS renders "then she ceased bearing." PNEUMA renders "And she stopped bearing children." The Hebrew *'āmad* with *min* means to cease from an action. "Ceased from bearing" (EMET) preserves the idiomatic preposition. The council adopts "and she ceased from bearing" — a temporary pause, not permanent barrenness, as the subsequent narrative will show.

---

### The Theology of This Chapter — The Overlooked One

EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all, independently, identified the same theological movement in this chapter's closing section: the LORD's attention is drawn not to the beloved wife but to the overlooked one. PNEUMA's formulation is adopted as the council's theological summary: "What is theologically significant is the subject of this sentence: *the LORD saw*. The LORD's attention is drawn not to the beloved, the chosen, the celebrated — but to the overlooked one. To the woman who is there because she was substituted for her sister in the dark. This divine noticing — *wayyar' YHWH* — will be the engine of the rest of the chapter. God opens Leah's womb. He sees what Jacob cannot or will not see."

LOGOS's observation on Leah's sons is also adopted: the four naming speeches escalate — complaint (Reuben: seen), to petition (Simeon: heard), to longing (Levi: joined), to pure doxology (Judah: praised) — and the fourth name, the one that breaks the pattern of instrumental reasoning and becomes pure thanksgiving, names the son from whom the entire covenantal and royal line descends.

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### 29:1 — The *Vayyissa' Raglav* Idiom

**The issue:** How to render the unique Hebrew idiom *wayyissa' Ya'aqov raglav* (וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו — "and Jacob lifted his feet") for the act of setting out on a journey.

**EMET's position:** "And Jacob lifted his feet [וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו] and went." Preserve word-for-word with the transliterated Hebrew in brackets. The idiom is unique; it should not be smoothed.

**LOGOS's position:** "Then Jacob lifted his feet and journeyed." Preserve the idiom with "journeyed" to convey the sense of a significant journey, not just a step.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Jacob picked up his feet and traveled east." "Picked up his feet" is slightly more English-idiomatic while retaining the physical gesture; "traveled east" names the direction.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *ēre de Iakōb tous podas autou* (ἦρε δὲ Ιακωβ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ) — "Jacob lifted his feet" — preserves the Hebrew idiom exactly, confirming it as an ancient reading.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "And Jacob lifted his feet and journeyed" (combining LOGOS's "journeyed" with the Hebrew-faithful "lifted his feet" and the narrative-sequential "And"). All three translators agreed the idiom must be preserved in recognizable form; the only question was the exact English idiom. The LXX's literal rendering of "lifted his feet" is decisive testimony for preserving the unusual Hebrew formulation. PNEUMA's "picked up his feet" is the most English-natural variant and is not wrong; the council judges that the archaic quality of "lifted his feet" is a feature worth preserving here, not a defect, since the idiom's strangeness signals to the reader that something distinctive is being said. The verse is flagged ⚠️.

---

### 29:17 — Leah's Eyes: *Rakkot* — "Weak" vs. "Soft"

**The issue:** Whether the adjective *rakkot* (רַכּוֹת) describing Leah's eyes should be rendered as a deficiency ("weak," "dim," "dull") or as a neutral/positive quality ("soft," "tender," "gentle").

**EMET's position:** Render "weak" — the LXX Vaticanus reads *astheneis* (weak/feeble), the Vulgate reads *lippis* (bleary/weak-eyed), and the contrast structure of the verse (Leah's *rakkot* eyes **but** Rachel's full beauty) supports a disadvantageous reading. "Weak" is the majority ancient witness.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "soft" — *rakh* is the Hebrew root and "soft" preserves the MT's own semantic range without importing the LXX's resolution of the ambiguity. The reader should be left to interpret "soft eyes" for themselves. LOGOS also notes that bright, gleaming eyes were the ancient beauty standard; "soft" could imply eyes that lacked the prized quality of luminosity.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "soft" — the ambiguity is real and the translator should not resolve it. Both readings (defect and positive quality) are defensible; "soft" is deliberately neutral and allows both to stand.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *astheneis* (ἀσθενεῖς) — "weak, feeble, sickly" — clearly negative.

**Vulgate:** *lippis* — "bleary-eyed, watery-eyed" — clearly negative.

**Targum Onqelos:** *yaeyyatan* ("beautiful, lovely") — clearly positive. The sole ancient version favoring the positive reading.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "weak" (EMET, with SOPHIA's casting judgment). The weight of ancient evidence — LXX Vaticanus and Vulgate both reading the term negatively, against only Onqelos for the positive — tips the balance toward "weak." The narrative contrast structure (Leah's eyes *but* Rachel's full beauty) also favors "weak" as the contrast to Rachel's striking appearance. However, the council acknowledges that "soft" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) is a legitimate reading of the Hebrew root and preserves an ambiguity that is genuinely present in the MT. Readers who find "soft" more accurate to their reading of the Hebrew are not wrong. The verse is flagged ⚠️.

---

### 29:31 — "Leah Was Hated" (*shĕnu'āh*) vs. "Leah Was Unloved"

**The issue:** Whether *shĕnu'āh* (שְׂנוּאָה, Qal passive participle of *śānē'* — "to hate") should be rendered at full strength ("hated") or softened to the idiomatic comparative sense ("unloved," "not loved").

**EMET's position:** Render "hated" — the Hebrew word is *śānē'* and the LXX Vaticanus reads *miseitai* (is hated), the strong Greek verb. The full force of the word must not be translated away. Leah's condition is not mere indifference — it is the condition of the comparatively aversive, and "hated" correctly names the pain of that position.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "unloved" — the word *śānē'* functions idiomatically in Hebrew as a comparative-preference term (cf. Deuteronomy 21:15–17, where the "hated" wife is the less-preferred wife, not the despised one). Jacob did not actively hate Leah; v. 30 has stated that he "loved Rachel more than Leah." "Unloved" captures the idiomatic sense without making Jacob into a cruel man.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "unloved" — same reasoning. PNEUMA adds: "Jacob did not actively despise Leah; he simply did not love her the way he loved Rachel, and in the comparative world of polygamy, that difference registered as rejection."

**LXX Vaticanus:** *hoti miseitai Leia* (ὅτι μισεῖται Λεια) — "because Leah is hated" — follows the strong sense without softening.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "hated" (2–1, EMET and SOPHIA's judgment against LOGOS and PNEUMA). The LXX Vaticanus is the council's primary secondary source and it reads *miseitai* — the full-strength "is hated." The argument for "unloved" from Deuteronomy 21:15–17 is persuasive but does not nullify the stronger ancient textual witness. "Hated" preserves the text's unflinching account of Leah's condition; the reader who understands the comparative-preference idiom from the surrounding narrative (v. 30 has just stated Jacob "loved Rachel more than Leah") will understand "hated" as the idiomatic comparative. The narrative is more theologically powerful when the LORD's response in opening Leah's womb is set against the full weight of *śānē'* — it is the hated one whom God sees. The verse is flagged ⚠️. Both positions are documented; readers who prefer "unloved" are not reading against the Hebrew.

---

### 29:34 — "I Have Borne Him" vs. "I Have Fathered Him" (*yāladtî*)

**The issue:** Whether the Qal perfect *yāladtî* — first-person feminine, spoken by Leah — should be rendered "I have borne him" (the natural female-subject English rendering) or "I have fathered him" (the project's convention for the root *yālad* established in prior chapters for consistency across both male and female uses).

**EMET's position:** Render "I have fathered him three sons" — following the project convention that renders *yālad* consistently as "father/beget" in the Qal regardless of whether the subject is male or female, to maintain inter-chapter consistency.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "I have borne him three sons" — the subject is Leah and the natural English rendering of a woman giving birth is "borne," not "fathered." The project convention was designed for genealogical lists with male subjects; applying "fathered" to a female speaker is unnecessarily confusing.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "I have given him three sons" — a slightly freer rendering that avoids the "fathered"/"borne" debate by using the neutral "given."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "I have borne him three sons" (LOGOS, with PNEUMA's agreement, 2–1 against EMET's convention). The context is unambiguous — Leah is speaking, she has just given birth to three sons, and the natural English rendering of a mother's speech about her own children is "borne." Applying the project's *yālad* convention for genealogical lists to Leah's first-person birth speech produces an English anomaly that serves neither clarity nor faithfulness to the source. The convention is best applied where it was intended — genealogical formulas with male subjects. The LXX Vaticanus reads *etekon autō* ("I bore for him") — confirming the birthing sense. This is a minor divergence from the project convention, documented here for consistency review.
